# Stack4Things Upgrade Report

Author: Mohsen Ghalem

**Context**

After reviewing the three planned upgrades (ca\_service, iotronic-db, rabbitmq), a critical constraint was identified: the volumes unime\_iotronic\_db\_data and rabbitmq\_data cannot be deleted because the system is running in production. This changes the upgrade approach for two of the three services entirely.

# ca\_service

This change carries no production risk. The iotronic\_ssl volume only holds TLS certificates that are regenerated on every fresh start anyway. Replacing the debian:buster init container with pre-generated certs mounted from a local ./ssl/ directory removes the startup race condition without touching any persistent data.

**Problems with this:**

* Runs apt-get update && apt-get install openssl on every cold start  
* Needs internet access at startup  
* No healthcheck \-- crossbar and iotronic-wstun busy-wait on the files indefinitely  
* If apt mirror is slow or unreachable the whole stack hangs

**Improvement over the original**

|  | debian:buster (current) | cfssl (new) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| apt-get at startup | Yes | No |
| Needs internet at startup | Yes | No |
| Image size | \~120 MB \+ openssl download | \~50 MB, self-contained |
| Skips if certs already exist | No, reruns every time | Yes, checks first |
| Healthcheck possible | No | Can add test: \["CMD", "ls", "/etc/ssl/iotronic/crossbar.pem"\]  |

### Healthcheck on ca\_service

We have two others services that depending on the exsitance of the certificates, right now we’re having a endless whille loop untill we have the certifcate on place we can avoid that endless loop with a sample depend parameter we can add to the two service iotronic-wstun, crossbar,  containers

Before :

|      set \-x  \# DEBUG: Mostra i comandi eseguiti         echo "\[INFO\] Verifica permessi certificati..."         ls \-l /var/lib/iotronic/ssl         while \[ \! \-e /var/lib/iotronic/ssl/iotronic\_CA.pem \] || \[ \! \-e /var/lib/iotronic/ssl/crossbar.key \]; do           echo "\[DEBUG\] Certificati mancanti:"           ls \-l /var/lib/iotronic/ssl           sleep 2         done  |
| :---- |

After :

| depends\_on:       ca\_service:         condition: service\_healthy     entrypoint: \[ "/bin/sh", "-c" \] |
| :---- |

Note : we removed only the non-needed loop but we kept the other configuration logic on the both services

## Apply the changes :

1- Recreate only the three changed services

docker compose up \-d \--no-deps \--force-recreate ca\_service crossbar iotronic-wstun

![][image1]

2- Follow the logs  
docker compose logs \-f ca\_service crossbar iotronic-wstun

After seeing the logs I found that the two services (crossbar, iotronic-wstun) are crashing  
The problem was on the files does not exists yet on the volume  
so after running :  
docker exec ca\_service ls \-la /etc/ssl/iotronic/  
![][image2]  
I confirm that The volume still has the old files from the debian:buster run (old version). ca\_service saw them, skipped generation, and never created crossbar-key.pem or iotronic\_CA-key.pem that cfssl would produce.  
The old filenames (crossbar.key, iotronic\_CA.key) are exactly what crossbar and wstun were originally using.  
Here we have two solutions to apply is either to use the old generated certificates or to remove the current ones and force to start generating  a fresh certificates.

- First we delete the current certifcates  
  docker exec ca\_service sh \-c "rm /etc/ssl/iotronic/\*"  
- We restart the service and wait up to 10 seconds   
  docker compose restart ca\_service  
- Confirm crossbar-key.pem and iotronic\_CA-key.pem exist  
  docker exec ca\_service ls /etc/ssl/iotronic/  
  ![][image3]  
- After confirming we can restart the crossbar and iotronic-wstun services  
  docker compose restart crossbar iotronic-wstun  
  ![][image4]  
- Now we can check the serivces logs are running perfectly with the new certifcate service upgrade  
  ![][image5]  
  ![][image6]

iotronic-db  
Upgrading from mariadb:focal (MariaDB 10.7.3) to mariadb:11 is not possible by just changing the image tag when the data volume exists. MariaDB 11 will refuse to open a data directory created by MariaDB 10.7.3 due to binary format incompatibility. Attempting the upgrade without a dump will result in the container failing to start, taking down Keystone, Conductor, and Wagent with it.  
So the jump is from **10.7.3 to 11.x**. MariaDB's supported upgrade path requires going through **10.11 (LTS) first**, then to 11\. Jumping straight to 11 from 10.7 is not supported and will likely corrupt the data directory.

| mariadb:focal (10.7.3) \--\> mariadb:10.11 \--\> mariadb:11 |
| :---- |

Before anything else, take the dump now while everything is healthy:

| docker exec iotronic-db mysqldump \\   \-uroot \-punime \\   \--all-databases \\   \--single-transaction \\   \--routines \\   \--triggers \\   \--events \\   \> backup\_$(date \+%Y%m%d\_%H%M%S).sql |
| :---- |

Then we verify our backup exists  before we start upgrading

| \# check it is not empty wc \-l backup\_\*.sql \# check all three databases are in there grep "^-- Current Database" backup\_\*.sql  |
| :---- |

![][image7]  
Dump is good. 3812 lines, all three databases captured (iotronic, keystone, unime).

### Step 01 : upgrade to 10.11 first

First we need to stop the dependent services, change the image then we restart:

| \# stop everything that depends on the db docker compose stop keystone iotronic-conductor iotronic-wagent iotronic-ui lightning-rod \# stop the db docker compose stop iotronic-db  |
| :---- |

![][image8]  
After that we change the image in *docker-compose.yml* to *image: mariadb:10.11*, Also we fix *mysqld\_safe* to *mysqld* in the command block at the same time

| \# before mysqld\_safe \--bind-address=0.0.0.0 \\             \--max\_connections=4096 \\             \--collation-server=utf8\_general\_ci \\             \--character-set-server=utf8 \\             \--innodb-buffer-pool-size=1G \\ \# after mysqld \--user=mysql \\        \--bind-address=0.0.0.0 \\        \--max\_connections=4096 \\        \--collation-server=utf8\_general\_ci \\        \--character-set-server=utf8 \\        \--innodb-buffer-pool-size=256M \\  |
| :---- |

*mysqld\_safe* is a legacy wrapper removed in newer MariaDB versions. *mysqld \--user=mysql* is the correct direct invocation. The buffer pool is reduced because on macOS/Docker the 1G value causes OOM issues (but we can keep it to 1G on different environment )

### Problem: the redo log is still "dirty" from 10.7

If you simply recreate the container at this point, MariaDB 10.11 will refuse to start:

| docker compose up \-d \--no-deps \--force-recreate iotronic-db docker compose logs \--tail=20 iotronic-db |
| :---- |

We will see this error:

| \[ERROR\] InnoDB: Upgrade after a crash is not supported. The redo log was created with MariaDB 10.7.3. You must start up and shut down MariaDB 10.7 or earlier. \[ERROR\] InnoDB: Plugin initialization aborted with error Generic error \[ERROR\] Aborting |
| :---- |

**Root cause:** Even though the 10.7.3 container was stopped with docker compose stop, the compose entrypoint script restarts mysqld internally when mysqladmin shutdown is called. When docker compose stop then sends SIGTERM, mysqld gets killed mid-restart, leaving the redo log in a "crash" state. MariaDB 10.11 refuses to do crash recovery across major versions.  
**Fix:** Run mariadb:focal directly (bypassing the entrypoint) against the volume and do one clean shutdown:

| \# Stop the 10.11 container that failed docker compose stop iotronic-db \# Run 10.7 directly as PID 1 (no entrypoint restart loop) docker run \-d \\   \--name mariadb-clean-shutdown \\   \--entrypoint mysqld \\   \-v stack4things\_dockercompose\_deployment\_unime\_iotronic\_db\_data:/var/lib/mysql \\   mariadb:focal \\   \--user=mysql \\   \--innodb-buffer-pool-size=256M \\   \--skip-grant-tables \# Wait for it to be ready sleep 10 docker logs mariadb-clean-shutdown \--tail=5 |
| :---- |

Expected output:

| 2026-05-05 13:09:21 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: 10.7.3 started; log sequence number 1422979; transaction id 2228 ... 2026-05-07 13:09:22 0 \[Note\] mysqld: ready for connections. Version: '10.7.3-MariaDB-1:10.7.3+maria\~focal'  |
| :---- |

| \# Clean shutdown — SIGTERM goes directly to mysqld (PID 1\) docker stop \--timeout=30 mariadb-clean-shutdown docker logs mariadb-clean-shutdown \--tail=6 |
| :---- |

Expected output :

| 2026-05-05 13:09:39 0 \[Note\] mysqld (initiated by: unknown): Normal shutdown 2026-05-05 13:09:39 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: Shutdown completed; log sequence number 1423003; transaction id 2229 2026-05-05 13:09:39 0 \[Note\] mysqld: Shutdown complete |
| :---- |

After that we can just remove the temproray container we made   
docker rm mariadb-clean-shutdown  
Now all we need is to recreate the iotronic-db service and will upgrade cleanly

| docker compose up \-d \--no-deps \--force-recreate iotronic-db sleep 15 docker compose logs \--tail=30 iotronic-db |
| :---- |

Expected output :

| 2026-05-05 13:10:47 0 \[Note\] Starting MariaDB 10.11.16-MariaDB-ubu2204 ... 2026-05-05 13:10:47 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: Upgrading redo log: 96.000MiB; LSN=1423003 2026-05-05 13:10:47 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: log sequence number 1423003; transaction id 2228 ... Version: '10.11.16-MariaDB-ubu2204'  socket: '/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock'  port: 3306 mysqld is alive Il database 'unime' esiste già. MariaDB avviato correttamente\! |
| :---- |

### Step 02 : Upgrade to mariadb:11

As previous we need to do a clean shutdown of 10.11 before upgrading we going to use the same technique  which bypass the entrypoint to get a clean InnoDB shutdown:

| docker compose stop iotronic-db docker run \-d \\   \--name mariadb-clean-shutdown \\   \--entrypoint mysqld \\   \-v stack4things\_dockercompose\_deployment\_unime\_iotronic\_db\_data:/var/lib/mysql \\   mariadb:10.11 \\   \--user=mysql \\   \--innodb-buffer-pool-size=256M \\   \--skip-grant-tables sleep 10 docker stop \--timeout=30 mariadb-clean-shutdown docker logs mariadb-clean-shutdown \--tail=6 |
| :---- |

Expected output :

| 2026-05-05 13:12:41 0 \[Note\] mysqld (initiated by: unknown): Normal shutdown 2026-05-05 13:12:42 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: Shutdown completed; log sequence number 1423019; transaction id 2229 2026-05-05 13:12:42 0 \[Note\] mysqld: Shutdown complete |
| :---- |

Then we remove the *mariadb-clean-shutdown* container   
**Important note:**  
In mariadb:11, the mysql\* command names are removed. Verify what's available:  
*docker run \--rm mariadb:11 which mariadbd mariadb-admin mariadb mariadb-install-db*  
Output will be the path for the commands:

| /usr/sbin/mariadbd /usr/bin/mariadb-admin /usr/bin/mariadb /usr/bin/mariadb-install-db |
| :---- |

Now we can update the docker-compose.yml 

1. First we change the image tag to *mariadb:11*  
2. Second we change the test command from *mysqladmin* to *mariadb-admin*

Here’s a table shows commands upgrade :

| Old | New |
| :---- | :---- |
| mysql\_install\_db \--user=mysql \--ldata= | mariadb-install-db \--user=mysql \--datadir= |
| mysqld \--user=mysql \\ | mariadbd \--user=mysql \\ |
| while \! mysqladmin ping | while \! mariadb-admin ping |
| if mysql \-uroot | if mariadb \-uroot |
| mysql \-uroot ... \<\<EOF | mariadb \-uroot ... \<\<EOF |

After we upgrade all the commands with the new ones that matches the new version of *mariadb:11*  
We are ready to start again our DB container by recreating it

| docker compose up \-d \--no-deps \--force-recreate iotronic-db sleep 20 docker compose logs \--tail=40 iotronic-db |
| :---- |

Expected output :

| 2026-05-05 13:16:55 0 \[Note\] Starting MariaDB 11.8.6-MariaDB-ubu2404 ... 2026-05-05 13:16:55 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: End of log at LSN=1423019 2026-05-05 13:16:55 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: Upgrading the change buffer 2026-05-05 13:16:55 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: Upgraded the change buffer: 0 tablespaces, 0 pages 2026-05-05 13:16:55 0 \[Note\] InnoDB: Reinitializing innodb\_undo\_tablespaces= 3 from 0 ... Version: '11.8.6-MariaDB-ubu2404'  socket: '/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock'  port: 3306 mysqld is alive Il database 'unime' esiste già. MariaDB avviato correttamente\! |
| :---- |

Now we can verify the status of our DB 

![][image9]

### Step 03 : Bring the full stack up and verify

Now as we have the DB upgraded we need to start all the service using *docker compose up \-d*  
So the expected output should be 

| NAMES                IMAGE                                  STATUS iotronic-wagent      lucadagati/iotronic-wagent:latest      Up 44 seconds keystone             lucadagati/iotronic-keystone           Up 1 minute (healthy) iotronic-db          mariadb:11                             Up 30 minutes (healthy) iotronic-conductor   lucadagati/iotronic-conductor:latest   Up 7 seconds (healthy) rabbitmq             rabbitmq:3.7-management                Up 3 hours (healthy) ... |
| :---- |

### Problem: conductor fails with MultipleResultsFound

we can see that the iotronic-conductor (health: starting) and keeps restarting   
after doing some diagnosis

| docker exec iotronic-conductor tail \-20 /var/log/iotronic/iotronic-conductor.log |
| :---- |

 

Error you will see:

| CRITICAL iotronic-conductor Unhandled error: sqlalchemy.exc.MultipleResultsFound:   Multiple rows were found when exactly one was required ...   ragent \= self.dbapi.get\_registration\_wampagent()   .filter\_by(ragent=True, online=True) |
| :---- |

Root cause: The wampagents table has stale rows from old container runs, each container restart leaves an orphaned row with ragent=1, online=1, and the conductor requires exactly one such row.  
So the fix is to query the wampagents table and keep only the last updated at connection online and offline the rest

| docker exec iotronic-db mariadb \-uroot \-punime iotronic \\   \-e "SELECT id, hostname, ragent, online, updated\_at FROM wampagents WHERE ragent=1 AND online=1;" id  hostname      ragent  online  updated\_at 1   3b4b1af388c0  1       1       2026-05-05 10:31:20 2   abbe3a6e1311  1       1       2026-05-05 13:46:30 |
| :---- |

We keep the most recent row (highest id / latest updated\_at). Mark all others offline:

| docker exec iotronic-db mariadb \-uroot \-punime iotronic \\   \-e "UPDATE wampagents SET online=0 WHERE id=1;" |
| :---- |

Now we can restart the *iotronic-conductor*

| docker compose restart iotronic-conductor sleep 20 docker ps \--filter name=iotronic-conductor \--format '{{.Status}}' |
| :---- |

After we look to the logs we should find something like this 

| INFO iotronic.conductor.manager \[-\] Found registration agent: abbe3a6e1311 on wss://crossbar:8181/ |
| :---- |

Now our is upgrade complete. The full path mariadb:focal (10.7.3) → mariadb:10.11 → mariadb:11 is done, all services are healthy, and the production database unime is intact.

### Problem: Horizon: "Invalid service catalog: iot"

When: Attempting to create/manage IoTronic boards from the Horizon dashboard we get an error   
“Error: Invalid service catalog: iot bord”  
I did list all the service on the openstack I found many service have the same type

| docker exec keystone openstack service list |
| :---- |

Output :

| \+----------------------------------+----------+----------+ | ID                               | Name     | Type     | \+----------------------------------+----------+----------+ | 02a436483c2749f6a254f5286b303a91 | Iotronic | iot      | | 39c2adf75c14446ab8207011dd8ed220 | Iotronic | iot      | | 43d9836b73894dc59abbb3afe2dee29c | Iotronic | iot      | | 444b5b6e5a6e47729fd69775660c2c37 | Iotronic | iot      | | 7f7f2882a667418bbd8f1832d736d3ba | keystone | identity | \+----------------------------------+----------+----------+ |
| :---- |

The keystone container setup script runs on every container restart. Each run attempts to create the iot service. The creation fails with HTTP 409 Conflict (service already exists), but the script silently ignores the conflict and continues, leaving the previously created duplicate entry in place. After several container restarts (during the DB upgrade process), 4 identical iot service entries had accumulated. Horizon's service catalog lookup performs a *.one()* query expecting exactly one entry per service type, finding 4 raises an Invalid service catalog error for any IoTronic operation.

This was already visible in the keystone container logs at stack startup:

| Multiple service matches found for 'iot', use an ID to be more specific. Multiple service matches found for 'iot', use an ID to be more specific. Multiple service matches found for 'iot', use an ID to be more specific. |
| :---- |

The solution I applied was quite sample is to identify which service ID owns the actual endpoints:

| docker exec keystone openstack endpoint show 1c42deb592ef4060ab9f2c5bf80bfd78 2\>&1 | grep service\_id |
| :---- |

Output:

| | service\_id | 444b5b6e5a6e47729fd69775660c2c37 | |
| :---- |

After we defnied the service who owns the actual endpoints now we can delete the orphaned duplicate services

| for id in 02a436483c2749f6a254f5286b303a91 \\            39c2adf75c14446ab8207011dd8ed220 \\            43d9836b73894dc59abbb3afe2dee29c; do   openstack service delete $id done |
| :---- |

Now let’s verify again if we have only one service type iot

| docker exec keystone openstack service list |
| :---- |

| \+----------------------------------+----------+----------+ | ID                               | Name     | Type     | \+----------------------------------+----------+----------+ | 444b5b6e5a6e47729fd69775660c2c37 | Iotronic | iot      | | 7f7f2882a667418bbd8f1832d736d3ba | keystone | identity | \+----------------------------------+----------+----------+ |
| :---- |

Now as we see exactly 1 iot service with 3 endpoints (admin, public, internal), all pointing to http://iotronic-conductor:8812. Horizon resolved the service catalog correctly and the error was gone.

**Note in production envrinoment :**   
The root cause is the keystone setup script being idempotent for failures but not for side effects ,it silently continues after a 409, without cleaning up the partial state it just created. To prevent recurrence, either make the setup script check for existing services before creating them, or ensure the keystone container only runs the setup script once (e.g. via a flag file on a persistent volume).
